Chalkboard with Pros and Cons at the top. Under are the numbers 1-4.


After you finish this page, click Next to continue to Writing Details: Thesis Statement.


Week 4 | Writing Details: Thesis Statement

Thesis Statement
In simple words, a thesis statement is a sentence that tells the reader what the paper is about and what position it will support. It should be clear and able to be debated by others; a thesis is debatable when someone could disagree with you logically and reasonably. The thesis statement usually comes at the end of the first paragraph but can also be found in other parts of the paper.

A good thesis statement sets up what you will argue in your paper and ensures that your reader knows your position on the topic. It also helps keep you, as the writer, from getting lost in a confusing argument. Most importantly, a good thesis statement makes a statement. After all, it's called a thesis statement for a reason!

A thesis is not a question; it is not a list nor something that can be proven true or false. A strong thesis makes an argument or a claim you intend to support with evidence. A weak thesis either states an obvious fact or rephrases someone else's opinion.
